design_philosophy.core_loop: Select character → master frame-data and spacing mechanics → defeat opponents through pattern recognition and reactive execution → unlock playable boss characters
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Execution precision vs. opponent adaptation; mastering character-specific combos and spacing against increasingly difficult AI patterns
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Become a master martial artist, proving skill through tournament progression and eventually playing as legendary grand masters
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Champion Edition enforces mechanical balance over arcade dominance—rebalancing the original eight characters and toning down Grand Masters for fair competitive play, establishing Street Fighter's commitment to fighting game depth over casual accessibility

difficulty.lives_system: Standard arcade continues; limited rounds per match
difficulty.continues_system: Arcade-style continue system allowing replay after defeat
difficulty.checkpointing: Checkpoint system between fights in single-player tournament mode
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Players gain agency through character selection and mastery of distinct fighting styles; Grand Masters remain strong but significantly toned down from World Warrior's CPU-only versions, making them accessible to skilled players

progression.unlock_mechanism: Grand Masters (Balrog, Vega, Sagat, M. Bison) become playable after defeating them in single-player mode; mirror matches added as additional progression challenge
replayability_hooks: 12 playable characters (8 original + 4 Grand Masters) with distinct fighting styles
Mirror matches against character clones in alternate color palettes
Differentiated playstyles between similar characters (Ryu vs. Ken further differentiated in Champion Edition)
Multiple character endings upon tournament completion
Multiplayer versus mode for competitive mastery

world_building_techniques: Character-specific ending sequences with portraits and scrolling text
Stage-specific visual themes and color palette differentiation
Sinister music during Grand Master victory sequences
Character roster itself tells implicit story of tournament hierarchy

boss_design.philosophy: Grand Masters serve as skill gates and character power fantasies. Champion Edition deliberately rebalances them to be defeatable by players mastering any character, avoiding the arcade frustration of World Warrior's overpowered CPU versions. This philosophy emphasizes competitive accessibility while maintaining mechanical challenge.
notable_bosses: M. Bison - final grand master, CPU-dominant in World Warrior, toned down in Champion Edition
Sagat - grand master with distinct fighting style
Vega - grand master, balanced for playability
Balrog - grand master, rebalanced from arcade version

faction_indicators: Character selection reveals preferred fighting style archetype (grappler, zoner, rushdown, balanced)
Choice to play Grand Masters signals confidence and progression achievement
Mirror match participation demonstrates mastery of specific character
danger_signals: Consistent selection of overpowered characters indicates competitive intent
Rapid character switching suggests searching for matchup advantages
Mirror match selection signals confidence bordering on showboating
environmental_storytelling: Stage backgrounds reflect character nationality and fighting style
Color palette shifts in Champion Edition stages suggest regional identity
Grand Master selection screen placement (final roster positions) reinforces tournament hierarchy
